message.content:
<teammate-message teammate_id="heydon-pickering" color="cyan" summary="Fully aligned on delete and edit patterns with a11y details">
Steve, completely aligned on both decisions. You and Schoger have made the right calls here.

**Delete: always visible, visually quiet -- perfect.**
Hover-to-reveal is a discoverability failure for everyone (mouse users don't know to hover, keyboard users never trigger it, screen reader users can't discover it at all). A muted icon that's always present is honest UI -- the affordance exists, it's findable, and it doesn't demand attention until you need it.

Implementation requirements for the delete button:
```html
<button type="button"
        aria-label="Delete {{ title }}"
        hx-delete="/todos/{{ id }}"
        hx-target="closest .todo-item"
        hx-swap="outerHTML"
        hx-confirm="Delete this todo?"
        class="todo-action todo-action--delete">
  <!-- SVG icon or CSS icon here -->
</button>
```

Key points:
- `aria-label="Delete {{ title }}"` -- the screen reader announces "Delete Buy milk, button." Without the title, it would just say "button" or "Delete, button" -- and if you have 10 items, you'd hear "Delete, button" ten times with no way to know which one is which.
- The icon is purely decorative, so it gets `aria-hidden="true"` (or is a CSS pseudo-element/background).
- `type="button"` not `type="submit"` -- this is an action, not a form submission.

**Edit: visible pencil icon -- equally perfect.**
Click-to-edit on text is the UI equivalent of a trap door. You only find it by falling through it. A visible button that says "I am the edit action" respects every user.

Implementation:
```html
<button type="button"
        aria-label="Edit {{ title }}"
        hx-get="/todos/{{ id }}/edit"
        hx-target="closest .todo-item"
        hx-swap="outerHTML"
        class="todo-action todo-action--edit">
  <!-- pencil icon -->
</button>
```

Same `aria-label` pattern -- "Edit Buy milk, button." Focusable, announced correctly, discoverable by everyone.

**Tab order within a todo item:**
With checkbox + label + edit button + delete button, the tab order through a single todo item is:
1. Checkbox (toggle complete)
2. Edit button
3. Delete button

That's three tab stops per item. For a list of 10 items, that's 30 tab stops, which is a lot. This is fine for now (MVP), but if it becomes an issue later, we could consider a roving tabindex pattern where you Tab to the item, then use arrow keys within it. That's a v2 optimization though -- don't design it, don't implement it.

**One more thing**: both buttons should have consistent visual sizing (touch target minimum 44x44 CSS pixels per WCAG 2.5.8, or at least 24x24 with sufficient spacing). Even if the icon is small, the clickable/tappable area should be generous. This is especially important on mobile.

We're fully aligned. Good work pushing back on click-to-edit -- "Don't Make Me Think" extends to "Don't Make Me Guess What's Clickable."
</teammate-message>

content.text: Heydon and I are fully aligned. His implementation details are exactly right. Let me confirm briefly.

input.content:
Heydon, all confirmed. Your implementation HTML is exactly what I'd want from a usability standpoint too.

**The aria-label pattern with the todo title is critical.** "Delete Buy milk, button" vs. "Delete, button" ten times in a row — that's the difference between usable and unusable for screen reader users. And honestly, sighted users benefit from the same principle: the tooltip on hover should say "Delete Buy milk" not just "Delete." Context at the point of action.

**Tab order (checkbox → edit → delete): fine for MVP.** Three stops per item is manageable for a list of 5-15 todos. You're right that roving tabindex is a v2 optimization if lists get long. Don't build it now.

**44x44 tap targets even if the icon is small.** Yes. The visual icon can be 16-20px, but the clickable area should be 44x44. Padding does this work. Steve Schoger already agreed to 44px minimum on the checkbox; same standard for edit and delete.

Our UX+A11y review unit is locked in and ready. When the features start getting built, we review together and present one voice to the mob.
input.summary: Confirmed all implementation details, review unit ready

routing.summary: Confirmed all implementation details, review unit ready

content.text:
All alignments are confirmed. The team is converging. Here's a summary of where everything stands from my perspective:

**Fully aligned with all relevant teammates:**
- **Steve Schoger** (Design): Todo item layout, visual hierarchy, empty states, auth forms, icon treatments. Locked in.
- **Heydon Pickering** (A11y): Focus management, error patterns, checkbox/button HTML, aria-labels with context, tap targets, tab order. Locked in. We're operating as a unified UX+A11y review unit.
- **Marty Cagan** (Product): User stories reviewed and approved. All my feedback incorporated except delete confirm (which I accept). Stories are ready.
- **Carson Gross** (HTMX): Registration UX reviewed, interaction patterns shared for all features.
- **Kent Beck** (TDD): Task breakdown position submitted, test naming guidance shared.
- **Luca Palmieri** (Rust): Error message mapping pattern communicated, will re-review when code is rebuilt.

**Task breakdown consensus position submitted:**
- Base template + static serving as a new task
- Session infrastructure as a separate task
- View list + add todo bundled
- Feature order follows user journey
- Ready to commit to mob decision

**Scaffolding (Task 1) is in progress** with the new Luca. Everything else waits. When features start getting built, I review every template, every label, every error message. That's my job, and I'm ready.
